act: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 46 (Order restraining harassment); Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, Rules 25(11)(b)(i.1), 25(11.1)
title: Form 25F — Restraining Order
subtitle: Ontario Family Court — Family Law Act, s. 46
description: A court-issued restraining order under s. 46 of the Family Law Act to prevent harassment and communication between former spouses or partners. Completed by the court clerk after a judge grants the order. The user fills in the applicant details and the conduct being restrained; a judge signs the order. Registered on the Canadian Police Information Centre (CPIC) database. Disobeying this order is a criminal offence.

safetyFlag.message: If you or your children are in immediate danger, call 911. A restraining order takes time to obtain through the courts. If you need protection immediately, contact the police first.
safetyFlag.urgentMotion: If you need a restraining order urgently (without waiting weeks for a hearing), you can file an urgent motion WITHOUT NOTICE to the other party using Form 14 (Notice of Motion) and Form 14A (Affidavit). The judge can grant a temporary restraining order the same day.

steps.title: Safety Information
steps.subtitle: Read before continuing
steps.intro: Form 25F is the restraining order itself, signed by a judge. You need to apply for it first using a motion or application. This section explains what you need to do.

fields.label: Immediate Danger
fields.content: If you or your children are in immediate danger, call 911. Do not wait for a court order.

fields.label: Urgent Restraining Order (Without Notice)
fields.content: If you need protection quickly, you can file an urgent motion WITHOUT telling the other party first. File Form 14 (Notice of Motion) and Form 14A (Affidavit) explaining why you are afraid. A judge can grant a temporary restraining order the same day. You will also need to start or reopen a family court case with Form 8 (Application General).

fields.label: Which Law Applies — Form 25F
fields.content: Form 25F is used for restraining orders under section 46 of the Family Law Act (FLA). This applies to former spouses or partners in a family law case. It orders the restrained person to stop harassing or communicating with you. If your case involves children and the order is under the Children's Law Reform Act (CLRA), use Form 25G instead.

fields.label: CPIC Registration & Criminal Consequences
fields.content: Once granted, this order is registered on the Canadian Police Information Centre (CPIC) database. Any police officer in Canada can enforce it. Disobeying this order is a criminal offence punishable by fine or imprisonment. The restrained person can be arrested without a warrant under s. 495 of the Criminal Code.

fields.label: Do you already have a family court case open (a court file number)?
fields.helpText: If yes, your restraining order application will be added to your existing case. If no, you will need to file Form 8 (Application General) to start a new case at the same time.

fields.label: Starting a New Case
fields.content: Since you don't have an open case, you will need to file Form 8 (Application General) to start one. Hearth & Page will help you prepare both Form 8 and this restraining order application together.

steps.title: Court Information
steps.subtitle: Where the case is or will be filed

fields.label: Name of Court
options: Superior Court of Justice (Family Court Branch)

fields.label: Court File Number (if you already have one)

steps.title: Applicant & Respondent
steps.subtitle: Who is applying and who is being restrained
steps.intro: The applicant is the person seeking the restraining order (you). The respondent is the person you want restrained.

fields.label: Your Address for Service
fields.helpText: This address will appear on the court form. You may use a lawyer's address or a P.O. Box if you are concerned about the respondent knowing your location.

fields.label: Respondent Date of Birth
fields.helpText: Required for CPIC registration of the restraining order.

fields.label: Does the respondent have a lawyer?

steps.title: Conduct to be Restrained
steps.subtitle: What you are asking the court to order
steps.intro: Describe the specific conduct you want the judge to order the respondent to stop. Be as specific as possible — a judge can only order what you ask for.

fields.label: I am asking the court to order the respondent not to:
Come to or near my home or residence
Come to or near my workplace
Come to or near my children's school or daycare
Contact or communicate with me (by any means including phone, text, email, or social media)
Contact or communicate with our children except as permitted by a parenting order
Describe the other conduct to be restrained
Specific addresses / locations to include in the order
fields.placeholder:
123 Main Street, Toronto, ON — my residence
456 School Road, Toronto, ON — children's school
fields.helpText: List all addresses the restraining order should cover. The more specific you are, the easier it is for police to enforce.

fields.label: Exclusion distance (metres) — how close the respondent must not come
fields.helpText: Common distances are 100-500 metres. Leave blank if you are not requesting a specific distance.

fields.label: Order Effective Date (usually the date the judge signs)
fields.helpText: Usually left for the judge to complete. Leave blank unless your lawyer advises otherwise.

fields.label: Order Expiry Date (if not indefinite)
fields.helpText: Restraining orders can be permanent or time-limited. If you want a permanent order, leave blank. If you want a specific end date (e.g., until children finish high school), enter it here.

fields.label: Are you requesting a permanent (indefinite) restraining order?

steps.title: Motion Type
steps.subtitle: With or without notice to the respondent
steps.intro: You must decide whether to give the respondent notice of your motion before the hearing, or to apply without notice for an emergency order.

fields.label: How are you bringing your motion?

options.label: With notice — I will serve the respondent before the hearing (standard process)
Without notice — I need an emergency temporary order immediately (the respondent will be served after)

fields.helpText: Most motions are made with notice. A motion without notice is only appropriate in urgent safety situations where giving notice could put you or your children at risk.

fields.label: Why are you asking for a motion without notice?
fields.placeholder: Describe the urgency and why giving notice to the respondent would put you or your children at risk.
fields.helpText: The judge needs to know why you cannot give notice. Describe recent incidents, threats, or danger.

fields.label: Describe the incidents of harassment or threatening behaviour
fields.placeholder:
On [date], the respondent...
On [date], the respondent...
fields.helpText: Provide specific dates, locations, and descriptions of each incident. This information will go in your Form 14A (Affidavit) which must be filed with this motion.

fields.label: Have you called the police about any of these incidents?

steps.title: Required Documents & Next Steps
steps.subtitle: What to file with the court
steps.intro: Form 25F is the order itself — it is signed by the judge, not by you. You must file supporting documents to get the order granted.

fields.label: What to File with the Court
fields.content: To apply for a restraining order, file these documents together: (1) Form 8 (Application General) if you don't already have an open case, (2) Form 14 (Notice of Motion) stating you are asking for a restraining order under s. 46 of the Family Law Act, (3) Form 14A (Affidavit General) describing the incidents of harassment, (4) A blank Form 10 (Answer) for the respondent, (5) CPIC Restraining Order Information Form (available only at the courthouse counter). If your motion is WITH notice, also file Form 14C (Confirmation of Motion) at least 3 business days before the hearing.

fields.label: I understand I need to file Form 8 (Application General) to start or reopen my court case.
I understand I need to file Form 14 (Notice of Motion) and Form 14A (Affidavit) to support my request.
I understand the CPIC Restraining Order Information Form is available only at the courthouse counter — not online.
Support Resources
fields.content: If you are leaving an abusive relationship, contact a Family Court Support Worker at your local courthouse (free service). You can also call the Assaulted Women's Helpline at 1-866-863-0511 (24 hours). Duty counsel (free legal advice) is available at every Ontario family courthouse on motion days.
